content:
Member-only story

15 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A toggle only looks like a switch. role=”switch” and aria-checked are what make a screen reader actually say so.Run an accessibility audit on most sites and you’ll see the same fixes. Add alt text. Bump the contrast. Make the font bigger. Ship it.
None of that tells you if a blind person can actually check out on your site. None of it tells you if someone using only a keyboard can open your modal, close it, and get back to where they were. None of it tells you if your “loading” state is silent to anyone who can’t see the spinner.
Link for free (non-member reader)
This is the other half of accessibility. The half that never shows up in a design file, never gets caught by a Lighthouse score, and quietly decides whether your app works for millions of people or just looks like it does.
Don’t take my word for it. Tab through the demo yourself. Try it with nothing but your keyboard.

Here’s the whole thing, with real code.
How a blind person actually uses your site
